channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Iran War Live Updates: U.S. and Tehran Send Mixed Signals on Emerging Peace Agreement
item.description: President Trump said it would be signed on Sunday, but Iran’s Foreign Ministry cautioned that the timeline could be slower.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times

item.title: Japan Is Running Out of Royals. Are More Men the Answer?
item.description: Japan’s legislature is drafting a plan to allow the imperial family to adopt distant male relatives. But some in Japan would prefer a female emperor.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández and Hisako Ueno
item.media:description: Emperor Naruhito of Japan, center, with Empress Masako and other royal family members at a garden party in April in Tokyo.

item.title: Germany and Japan Are Rearming Again, 80 Years After World War II
item.description: After becoming allies to disastrous effect in the 1940s, Berlin and Tokyo are finding new reasons to team up — including rebuilding their militaries.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley and Javier C. Hernández
item.category: Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Shinjiro Koizumi, Japan’s defense minister, and his German counterpart, Boris Pistorius, at a naval base in Yokosuka, Japan, in March. The countries have been building up their militaries.

item.title: With a Deal Seemingly Close, the U.S. Faces an Iran More Willing to Withstand Pressure
item.description: Iran’s new, more militaristic leaders have already survived the worst that America and Israel can deliver, and seem readier to take risks.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of the Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps in April at a government-organized march in Tehran.

item.title: Russia Is Rich in Ballistic Missiles. Ukraine Is Short of Ways to Stop Them.
item.description: Ukraine is running out of American-made Patriot air-defense interceptors, and is pleading for more.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Kulish, Kim Barker, Siobhán O’Grady and Lara Jakes
item.media:description: Ukrainian service members next to a launcher of a Patriot air-defense system, in an undisclosed location, in 2024.

item.title: At the Canadian Screen Awards, the Industry Steps Outside Hollywood’s Shadow
item.description: No longer content with being just “Hollywood North,” the Canadian screen industry is asserting itself and telling its own stories.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff and Cole Burston
item.media:credit: Cole Burston for The New York Times
item.media:description: The actor Robbie Graham-Kunz speaking with fans outside the 14th Canadian Screen Awards in Toronto.

item.title: A Tren de Aragua Leader Is Killed in a Joint Strike, U.S. and Venezuela Say
item.description: A strike this week in Venezuela killed a gang leader known as Niño Guerrero who was wanted in the United States, officials in both countries said.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump said the operation had been conducted in close consultation with the new Venezuelan government.

item.title: A Family Ravaged by Land Mines in Myanmar
item.description: Bu Ri lost a leg to a mine in Myanmar decades ago. More recently, six other members of his family have suffered a similar fate or worse, in a sign of the devastation wreaked by civil war.
item.dc:creator: Richard C. Paddock

item.title: Brazilians Revive a World Cup Ritual to Cheer on Their National Team
item.description: After years of dampened enthusiasm about the World Cup, Brazilians are excited about their national team again — and they’re showing it by painting the streets.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova and Dado Galdieri
item.media:credit: Dado Galdieri for The New York Times

item.title: Should Switzerland Cap Its Population at 10 Million? Voters Will Decide.
item.description: One of the world’s richest countries is about to hold a referendum on a measure that would curb migration and most likely the economy. It is being sold in warm tones.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.media:description: Campaign posters ahead of the population cap vote. President Trump’s face is on a no poster, with the slogan, “Now, of all times, a break with Europe?” The yes slogan shown is “Protect Switzerland.”

item.title: What to Know About the Ebola Outbreak
item.description: Aid agencies are racing to help health workers in the Democratic Republic of Congo. The virus is known to have killed at least 140 people, but the true toll may be far higher.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel, Amelia Nierenberg and Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: At the Mongbwalu General Hospital in Ituri Province, Democratic Republic of Congo. That province, in the northeast of the country, is where the current outbreak was first identified.

item.title: U.S. Plan Is Said to Pull a Third of Fighter Jets It Provides NATO for Europe
item.description: The plan, outlined by officials and in a written document, provides rare clarity about the extent to which the Trump administration intends to reduce its commitment to NATO.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze and Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: An American F-16 jet at Andravida air base, Greece, last year.

item.title: A Dangerous Limbo Leaves Iran, and the World, Between Peace and War
item.description: Since announcing a nominal cease-fire two months ago, Iran, Israel and the U.S. have remained locked in low-intensity violence that has become a new normal.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler and Anton Troianovski
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: In Tehran on Monday, a billboard featuring the Iran theocracy’s first two supreme leaders loomed over passers-by.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang, Jonathan Swan and Abdi Latif Dahir

item.title: In Venezuela, a 91-Year-Old Weaver Sticks to Traditions
item.description: Though electric machines are now standard, the Venezuelan weaver Margarita Mora has clung to a mix of ancestral Indigenous and Spanish practices to create surprisingly modern work.
item.dc:creator: Silvia Benedetti and Andrea Hernández
item.media:credit: Andrea Hernandez for The New York Times

item.title: Top Haitian Security Official Kidnapped
item.description: A security expert who had recently become chief of staff to the new defense minister was abducted, the latest example of violence gripping the country.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles
item.media:description: Cars burned by armed gangs in downtown Port-au-Prince, Haiti, in January.

item.title: 5 Children Are Killed After Van and S.U.V. Collide in Rural Ontario
item.description: The children — four girls and one boy — were among 10 people in the van at the time of the crash. An infant was seriously injured, the police said.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Swan and Abdi Latif Dahir

item.title: Unfinished Business
item.description: We like it when projects are completed, but what if we could abide comfortably in the toiling and striving it takes to get them there?
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch
item.category: Basilica i Temple Expiatori de la Sagrada Familia (Barcelona, Spain)

item.title: An Honored Canadian Journalist on the Importance of Investigative Reporting
item.description: Our Times colleague Susanne Craig was back home in Canada to be honored for her extraordinary career, which began in Calgary, Alberta, her hometown.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.category: Lucky Loser: How Donald Trump Squandered His Father's Fortune and Created the Illusion of Success (Book)

item.title: The Scientific Quest for Perfect World Cup Pitch
item.description: Every match must be played on natural grass that gives players as consistent a surface as possible, no matter the venue. Cue the years of sod studies.
item.dc:creator: Alan Burdick

item.title: Ebola Comes for Congo’s Most Vulnerable Children
item.description: The arrival of a sick newborn at Saint Nicholas Orphanage in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo set off an Ebola outbreak that quickly spread among the country’s most vulnerable. Local health authorities are now monitoring the children’s home, but at least two babies have already died.
item.dc:creator: Bethlehem Feleke, Michael Anthony Adams, Yasu Tsuji and Jon Hazell

item.title: Afghans Hold Rare Public Protests Against Taliban Rules
item.description: The United Nations said it was “deeply concerned” about the arrests of dozens of women, and reported that two people were killed in protests organized to support them.
item.dc:creator: Yaqoob Akbary and Zane Irwin
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: In Herat, Afghanistan, on Monday.

item.title: U.S. Says Iran Cease-Fire Deal ‘Very Close’
item.description: A senior administration official said the two sides were “not quite at the finish line yet.”
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Swan and David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Western Australia Is Battling a Mouse Plague
item.description: For months, mice have been found in tea kettles, crunched by car tires and even appeared in people’s beds. In one town, the end might be in sight.
item.dc:creator: Laura Chung

item.title: Canada Has Its Elbows Up and Arms Wide Open at the World Cup
item.description: Facing President Trump’s threats, many Canadians have taken a defensive stance to their neighbor. But now Canada is playing co-host with the United States and Mexico, and welcoming the world.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Ian Willms for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fans celebrating a goal to tie the game during a public viewing of the match on Friday in Toronto.

item.title: As Speculation Swirls About the Emerging Iran Deal, Here’s What We Know
item.description: A “memorandum of understanding” between the two sides is under discussion. Both sides are keen to frame it as a victory.
item.dc:creator: Adam Rasgon and Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Spain’s Church Hopes Pope Trip Will Stem Secular Tide
item.description: Leo XIV’s visit to Spain, which ended on Friday, has spurred expectations among Catholic leaders of a spiritual revival.
item.dc:creator: Jason Horowitz
item.media:description: Pope Leo XIV arrives at Plaza de Lima in Madrid on Saturday, June 6, for a prayer vigil with young people on the first day of a seven-day visit to mainland Spain and the Canary Islands.

item.title: Scientists Race to Test Treatments as Ebola Outbreak Widens
item.description: Trials are beginning on several drugs that have shown promise in preliminary studies against the virus that is causing the current outbreak.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer and Stephanie Nolen
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A health worker in the town of Mongbwalu, Democratic Republic of Congo, collecting medication for patients suspected of having Bundibugyo virus last month.

item.title: Israel Is Building Army Base in Jenin, Flouting 1990s Pact With Palestinians
item.description: Critics say the move would help the Israeli military protect increased settlement activity near Palestinian population centers in the West Bank.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger and Adam Rasgon
item.media:credit: Afif Amireh for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Israeli military has held part of Jenin, in the occupied West Bank, since January of last year.

item.title: Families of Air India Crash Victims Seek Answers One Year On
item.description: Grieving relatives returned to Ahmedabad to honor the 260 lives lost in the June 2025 disaster, but investigators have not yet released a report into the cause.
item.dc:creator: Pragati K.B., Anupreeta Das and Hari Kumar
item.media:description: A grieving relative at the site of last year’s Air India Flight 171 crash, where family members of those killed in the disaster held a vigil.

item.title: Despite Talk of an Iran Peace Deal, Lebanon’s War Grinds On
item.description: Israeli strikes on Friday left Lebanon out of sync with a cautious optimism taking hold elsewhere in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward and Abdi Latif Dahir
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Residents survey damage following an Israeli airstrike in the southern port city of Tyre on Friday.

item.title: Japan Pushes Back on Trump’s Anime Posts
item.description: The use of anime imagery online by President Trump and his administration has strained diplomatic ties with Japan and drawn criticism from creators and fans.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon and Hisako Ueno
item.media:credit: Paco Freire/SOPA Images, via LightRocket, via Getty Images
item.media:description: A Trump social media post from Saturday depicted himself dressed as the protagonist from the popular anime series “Naruto.”

item.title: JR Conjures a Mountainous Illusion Out of the Pont Neuf in Paris
item.description: Four decades after the conceptual artist Christo draped the Pont Neuf with fabric, JR, a French street artist, offers his own trick of the eye.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler and Elliott Verdier
item.media:credit: Elliott Verdier for The New York Times
item.media:description: The bridge has been wrapped with fabric that was then inflated to create a craggy appearance.

item.title: Anti-Immigrant Riots after Belfast Stabbing Leave City on Edge
item.description: In two nights of violence in Northern Ireland after a brutal stabbing, people were targeted because of their skin color, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:description: Attempting to clear protesters in Belfast, Northern Ireland, on Wednesday evening.

item.title: With iPhones and Faxes, David Hockney Embraced Tech
item.description: Polaroids and photocopiers also gave the artist possibilities for creating in forms vastly different from his paintings.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: “Mulholland Drive, June 1986,” a homemade print from a photocopier.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak, Farnaz Fassihi and Adam Rasgon

item.title: Bring the Kids and Grab Some Barbecue: A Day at the Ukrainian Drone Races
item.description: Competitions that give soldiers a brief break from the front have a festival-like atmosphere, a mix of potentially deadly weapons and frolicking children.
item.dc:creator: Maria Varenikova
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times
item.media:description: An audience watched the Wild Drones military drone-flying competition in Truskavets, Ukraine, last month.

item.title: A Globe-Spanning Collection of Children’s Art May Lose Its Home
item.description: For 40 years, a museum in Norway has showcased art by children from around the world, even toddlers. Now it has lost the state support it depended on.
item.dc:creator: Henrik Pryser Libell and Lynsey Chutel
item.category: International Museum of Children's Art (Oslo, Norway)

item.title: ‘Operation Pushkin’: Paris Trial Puts Spotlight on Rare-Book Heists
item.description: One by one, valuable works by Russian masters like Pushkin and Gogol were disappearing from libraries across Europe. Now six defendants are being prosecuted.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:description: A sculpture of Alexander Pushkin made of paper at a bookstore in St. Petersburg, Russia, this week.

item.title: Drones Flown Over North Korea Were Part of Martial Law Plot by Former South Korean President
item.description: A court ruled that Yoon Suk Yeol, the impeached former president, had sought to stir up instability to justify his bid for authoritarian rule in 2024.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:description: Former President Yoon Suk Yeol of South Korea, center, arriving for a hearing to review his arrest warrant in Seoul in 2025.

item.title: China Arrests U.S. Scholar on Spying Charge
item.description: The arrest of U Min Zin, who did graduate studies at U.C. Berkeley and directs a research group on Myanmar, took place soon after President Trump met with Xi Jinping in China.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong

item.title: Princess Bha of Thailand, Second in Line to the Throne, Dies at 47
item.description: Ever since she collapsed in a park three and a half years ago, speculation has swirled in Thailand about who could become the next monarch.
item.dc:creator: Sui-Lee Wee
item.media:description: Princess Bha of Thailand greeting royalists during a public appearance in Bangkok in 2020.

item.title: Whipsawed Between Fear and Relief, Iranians Hope for War’s End
item.description: In addition to concerns about their safety in the event of another all-out war, many Iranians worry about the country’s economy further collapsing if the conflict remains in limbo.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tehran on Monday.

item.title: Trump, in Latest Pivot, Retracts Threat to Strike Iran Again and Widen the War
item.description: Mr. Trump said that Iran was close to signing a peace deal. So far, weeks of talks have failed to produce an agreement.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak, Anupreeta Das, Michael Levenson and Jonathan Swan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump at the White House on Thursday, where he said a peace agreement with Iran was close at hand.

item.title: As World Cup Begins, Mexico City Becomes a Global Stage
item.description: Supporters of the national team gathered inside and outside the stadium for the opening of the games, celebrating the start of a tournament years in the making.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fans outside the Mexico City stadium known colloquially as Estadio Azteca before the opening match of the World Cup on Thursday.

item.title: The SpaceX I.P.O. Rocket
item.description: Elon Musk and people in his orbit are about to get much, much richer.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times

item.title: U.S. Blocks Deal by Florida-based Vanguard Energy to Supply Fuel to Cuba
item.description: The deal to ship 250,000 barrels of fuel to Cuba could have eased an energy crisis. But the Trump administration says Vanguard Energy lacks the authorization to proceed.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero
item.media:description: A blackout in Havana on Wednesday.

item.title: Security Is Tight as a Mexico Win Opens a Historic World Cup
item.description: As the largest World Cup kicked off in Mexico City on Thursday, the security around Estadio Azteca was strict, as law enforcement officers held off protesters.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner
item.media:credit: Cesar Rodriguez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Soccer fans in Mexico City encountered tight security on Thursday as Mexico and South Africa prepared to kick of the first of 104 World Cup matches.

item.title: 13 Afghan Civilians Killed in Airstrikes on Pakistan Border, U.N. Says
item.description: Pakistan said its airstrikes targeted militant camps. The United Nations confirmed reports by the Taliban government that mostly women and children were killed.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin, Safiullah Padshah and Zia ur-Rehman
item.media:description: Prayers offered for the victims of Wednesday’s airstrike in Afghanistan’s Khost Province.

item.title: Scientists Measure Earth’s Vast Underground Fungal Webs
item.description: With machine learning and a high-resolution imaging robot, scientists measured and mapped the extent of Earth’s carbon circulatory system.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes

item.title: Netanyahu Aide Charged With Endangering Israeli Security Over Document Leak
item.description: Jonatan Urich, a media adviser to the Israeli prime minister, was accused of passing classified intelligence about negotiations with Hamas to a German newspaper.
item.dc:creator: Natan Odenheimer
item.media:description: Jonatan Urich, a media adviser to Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, in August.

item.title: Refugees’ Numbers Drop, but Many Return to Turmoil at Home, U.N. Says
item.description: There were nearly 118 million forcibly displaced people in 2025, slightly fewer than in the previous year, the United Nations refugee agency said.
item.dc:creator: Nick Cumming-Bruce
item.category: United Nations High Commission for Refugees
item.media:description: Displaced people at a campsite in Lebanon in early April. Israel’s military offensive there had driven more than a million people from their homes by mid-May, the United Nations said.

item.title: NOAA Issues El Nino Advisory
item.description: The global weather pattern threatens to worsen floods and heat waves already intensifying due to climate change. But it may also mean fewer hurricanes.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.media:description: Drought-stressed wheat in a field in Kansas last month.

item.title: Iran’s Attacks on Gulf States Underscore Targeted Countries’ Dependence on U.S.
item.description: The targeted countries host sprawling American military bases and thousands of personnel at a time when the United States is waging war against Iran.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mural in Tehran, seen on Monday.

item.title: In the U.K., a Violent Cycle: Hateful Attacks, Right-Wing Agitation and Riots
item.description: Two stabbings have fueled violent protests in England and Northern Ireland in the last 10 days, fanned online by right-wing voices. In Britain and across Europe, it’s a grim pattern.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Protests north of Belfast, Northern Ireland, on Wednesday in response to a stabbing attack two days earlier.

item.title: Israel Set to Rapidly Expand West Bank Settlement
item.description: In a major push, the government is rushing to place temporary housing at about 60 empty sites in the Israeli-occupied West Bank before national elections this fall.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner
item.media:description: New prefabricated outpost settlement homes in May near the occupied West Bank village of Umm al-Khair and the Israeli settlement of Karmel on the hill.

item.title: Toronto Police Officer Fatally Shot While Investigating U.S. Consulate Shooting
item.description: The officer was shot on Thursday while investigating the firing of a gun outside the U.S. consulate in March, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Frank Gunn/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Police officers outside the United States Consulate in Toronto on March 10. Shots were fired at the building on that morning, the police said.
